action: Listen to this passage, then answer the questions from memory.
description: A short passage about the science of habits, followed by comprehension questions.
context: Active listening — absorbing and retaining spoken information — is a skill that improves with practice.
constraint_note: Read (or listen to) the passage once. Don't re-read before answering.

segments.text: Every habit follows the same loop: cue, routine, reward.
The cue is a trigger — a time, a place, an emotion. Your phone buzzing is a cue.
The routine is the behavior itself — checking the notification.
The reward is what your brain gets — a hit of novelty, connection, or information.
To change a habit, you don't fight the cue or the reward. You replace the routine.
Keep the same trigger, get the same reward, but change what you do in between.

questions.prompt: What are the 3 parts of the habit loop?
What example of a cue was given?
To change a habit, which part do you replace?
questions.answer: The routine (keep cue and reward the same)

action: Listen to this passage about decision fatigue, then answer from memory.
description: A passage about why we make worse decisions as the day goes on.
context: Longer passages test sustained attention. The questions require you to connect ideas, not just recall facts.

segments.text: A study of Israeli judges found something disturbing. Prisoners who appeared before the board in the morning received parole 70% of the time.
Those who appeared in the afternoon? Only 10%. Same crimes, same records. Different time of day.
This is decision fatigue. Every decision you make uses mental energy. By afternoon, the brain defaults to the easiest option — which for judges was denial.
This is why Steve Jobs wore the same outfit every day. One less decision. It's why you should make important decisions in the morning.
The solution isn't willpower. It's design. Reduce the number of decisions you make about things that don't matter, so you have energy for things that do.

questions.prompt: What was the parole rate in the morning vs afternoon?
Why did Steve Jobs wear the same outfit?
questions.answer: To eliminate one daily decision and save mental energy

questions.prompt: What's the solution to decision fatigue?
questions.answer: Design — reduce unimportant decisions to save energy for important ones

questions.prompt: What does the brain default to when fatigued?
questions.answer: The easiest option (path of least resistance)

action: Listen to this passage about how memory works, then answer from memory.
description: A passage about the science of memory formation and retrieval.
context: Ironic: this passage about memory tests your memory. The meta-awareness makes it stick better.
constraint_note: Listen once. The questions test both facts and understanding.

segments.text: Your brain doesn't store memories like a computer stores files. It reconstructs them every time you remember.
This means every act of remembering slightly changes the memory. The more you recall something, the more it shifts from the original.
There are three stages: encoding, storage, and retrieval. Most people fail at encoding — they never paid attention in the first place.
The most effective memory technique is spaced repetition — reviewing information at increasing intervals. Day 1, day 3, day 7, day 21.
Sleep is when memories consolidate. Learning something and then sleeping on it is literally how your brain files it away.
The worst way to study? Re-reading. The best way? Testing yourself. The struggle to recall is the learning.

questions.prompt: How does the brain store memories differently from a computer?
questions.answer: It reconstructs them each time (doesn't store fixed files)

questions.prompt: What are the 3 stages of memory?
What is spaced repetition?
What's the worst way to study? The best?
When do memories consolidate?

action: Listen to this passage about the 80/20 rule, then answer from memory.
description: A passage about the Pareto principle and how it applies to productivity.
context: Short, idea-dense passages train you to extract key concepts quickly.
constraint_note: Listen once. Focus on the core idea and the examples.

segments.text: In 1896, Italian economist Vilfredo Pareto noticed that 80% of Italy's land was owned by 20% of the population.
This pattern appears everywhere. 80% of your results come from 20% of your efforts. 80% of complaints come from 20% of customers.
For productivity, this means: find the 20% of your work that produces 80% of your impact. Do more of that. Eliminate or delegate the rest.
Most people spend their day on the 80% that barely matters. The skill is identifying and protecting the vital 20%.

questions.prompt: Who discovered the 80/20 pattern?
What does the 80/20 rule say about productivity?
questions.answer: 80% of results come from 20% of efforts

questions.prompt: What should you do with the vital 20%?
questions.answer: Do more of it. Eliminate or delegate the rest.

action: Listen to this passage about feedback loops, then answer from memory.
description: A more complex passage about positive and negative feedback loops in systems.
context: Complex passages with multiple concepts test your ability to hold and organize information.
constraint_note: This one is harder. Try to identify the structure: two types, examples of each, and the key insight.

segments.text: Every system runs on feedback loops. There are two types: positive and negative. The names are misleading — positive doesn't mean good.
A positive feedback loop amplifies change. Think of a microphone pointed at a speaker — the sound gets louder and louder until it screams. That's positive feedback.
In business: success attracts attention, which attracts customers, which creates more success. Social media works this way — viral content gets shown to more people, making it more viral.
A negative feedback loop stabilizes. Your thermostat is a negative feedback loop — when the room gets too hot, it turns off the heater. When it gets too cold, it turns it back on.
In your body: when blood sugar rises, insulin brings it down. When it drops, glucagon brings it up. Constant balancing.
The key insight: most problems come from positive feedback loops that nobody noticed. And most solutions come from adding a negative feedback loop — a check, a limit, a balancing force.

questions.prompt: What does a positive feedback loop do?
What does a negative feedback loop do?
Give one example of a positive feedback loop.
questions.answer: Microphone + speaker, viral content, success attracting more success

questions.prompt: Give one example of a negative feedback loop.
What's the key insight about problems and solutions?
questions.answer: Problems come from unnoticed positive loops; solutions come from adding negative (balancing) loops
